questions.question: What defines the topology of representation space? How do you know you are moving toward a simpler or more recognisable encoding without already having a pattern to compare against?
What compression criterion governs when a subgraph earns its own upper-layer node / library entry?
questions.resolution: Library entry is a byproduct of GA convergence. The GA fitness signal is label-conditioned reproducibility: given a fixed label, minimize variance in subgraph structures produced across different images with that label. Subgraphs that consistently appear under converged rules for a given label earn library entry. Frequency and MDL candidates superseded by this definition.

questions.question: How does the data system decide which candidate invariants to retain vs discard without an explicit confirmation step?
How does the rewrite system evolve itself — same feedback loop, but what is the fitness signal for its own logic?
questions.resolution: Fitness signal for the GA over rule graphs: given a fixed label, measure variance in subgraph structures produced across different images with that label. Lower variance = higher fitness. GA drives rules toward label-conditioned reproducibility. Full self-evolution mechanism (second system operating on rewrite system) remains deferred.

questions.question: Should genetic operations (mutation, crossover) be fixed primitives or should they also be representable as graph rewrites and therefore evolvable?

Is exact (hyper)graph isomorphism needed or is approximate similarity sufficient — for both library lookup AND rule application matching? The k-WL algorithm extends to hypergraphs but remains approximate. Must be resolved for both the recognition cascade and the transformation rule firing mechanism. Note: binary edge typing reduces isomorphism cost via regime-separated matching but does not fully resolve this question.

Are hypergraphs a minimum requirement for the representation? Standard graphs may fundamentally limit the representations discoverable — discrete systems have fragmented search spaces with unreachable configurations, as demonstrated by cellular automata rule evolution. Hyperedges allow multi-way structural relationships but raise isomorphism cost significantly. To be answered empirically via base prototype testing.
questions.resolution_path: Run base prototype on MNIST, inspect failure modes for representation fragmentation signal.

questions.question: What is the maximum hyperedge arity k? This is the primary architectural lever controlling the tradeoff between representational expressiveness and isomorphism tractability. Every rule application pays the matching cost — bounding k is necessary for the traveler to operate at runtime. Candidate constraint: k <= 3 or k <= 4 as a starting point.
questions.resolution_path: Contingent on q_07 — only relevant if hyperedges are warranted by empirical evidence.

questions.question: Does the layer hierarchy sufficiently compensate for the isomorphism cost of hypergraph matching? Higher layers compress structure and reduce graph size, but the per-node isomorphism problem remains regardless of layer depth. Is compression enough to keep rule firing tractable, or does the system need an additional mechanism — e.g. cached partial matches, bounded search, or approximate firing?
questions.resolution_path: Contingent on q_07 — measure matching cost profile in Phase 6 of prototype roadmap.

notes: Session 8: q_02 resolved — library entry defined as byproduct of GA convergence on label-conditioned reproducibility. q_04 partially resolved — fitness signal defined, self-evolution mechanism deferred. q_06 updated to note binary edge typing partial mitigation. q_07-q_09 marked as empirically resolved via prototype roadmap Phase 6.
